title: Finite and renewable resources
examTip: AQA wants you to distinguish between finite (non-renewable) and renewable resources, and to evaluate the sustainability of our resource use. Know examples of each type and the difference between crude oil as a feedstock vs a fuel. Sustainable development is a key evaluative concept.
introTips: Finite resources will run out — renewable ones can be replenished. That's the core distinction AQA tests.
Crude oil is unique because it's used both as a fuel AND as a feedstock for making chemicals and plastics — burning it as fuel wastes this value.
Sustainable development means meeting present needs without preventing future generations from meeting theirs — use this phrase in evaluation answers.
intro: Modern society depends heavily on resources that took millions of years to form and will run out within centuries if we continue using them at current rates. Chemistry has a central role in finding more sustainable alternatives — from renewable fuels to materials that replace finite ones. Understanding the difference between finite and renewable resources is the foundation.

keyPoints.heading: Finite vs renewable resources
keyPoints.content: Finite resources are those that exist in limited quantities and cannot be replaced on a human timescale — once used, they are gone. Examples: fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas — formed over millions of years), metallic ores (copper, iron, aluminium), and many non-metallic minerals. Renewable resources can be replenished naturally within a human lifetime. Examples: timber (can be replanted), water (recycled by the water cycle), wind and solar energy, food crops, wool and cotton. A key challenge is that modern chemistry and industry are heavily dependent on finite resources — particularly crude oil.
writeBullets: Finite resources: limited quantity, formed over millions of years, will eventually run out
Examples of finite: fossil fuels, metal ores (copper, iron), non-metallic minerals
Renewable resources: replenished naturally within a human lifetime
Examples of renewable: timber, wind, solar, water (water cycle), crops, wool, cotton
Modern industry is heavily dependent on finite resources — particularly crude oil

keyTerms.def: A natural resource that exists in limited quantities and cannot be replenished on a human timescale — e.g. fossil fuels, metal ores
A natural resource that can be replenished naturally within a human lifetime — e.g. timber, solar energy, crops
A finite fossil fuel that is both a source of energy and a feedstock for producing plastics, chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and other materials

keyPoints.memTip: Finite = formed over MILLIONS of years (can't wait for more). Renewable = replenished in a HUMAN LIFETIME (sustainable if managed well). Crude oil is both fuel AND feedstock — burning it is wasteful because you lose the chemicals too.

keyPoints.heading: Sustainable development and resource management
keyPoints.content: Sustainable development means meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. For resources, this means: using renewable resources instead of finite ones wherever possible; using finite resources efficiently and reducing waste; developing alternative materials (e.g. bioplastics from plant material instead of crude oil); recycling — recovering materials from products at the end of their life to reduce the need to extract new finite resources. Metals like copper, aluminium, and iron can all be recycled, reducing the need for mining ore (which requires energy and destroys habitats).
writeBullets: Sustainable development: meeting present needs without compromising future generations
Strategies: use renewable resources, use finite resources efficiently, recycle, develop alternatives
Recycling metals: reduces ore mining, saves energy, reduces habitat destruction
Bioplastics: made from plant material (renewable) instead of crude oil (finite)
Alternatives to finite fuels: solar, wind, hydrogen fuel cells

keyTerms.def: Development that meets present needs without preventing future generations from meeting their own needs
The recovery and reprocessing of materials from used products, reducing the need to extract new finite resources
Plastics made from plant-based (renewable) raw materials rather than crude oil

keyPoints.memTip: Sustainable development = don't use up what future generations need. Three strategies: Use renewables, Recycle, Reduce waste. Recycling is both economically and environmentally beneficial — it saves energy and reduces mining.

keyPoints.heading: Crude oil — feedstock not just fuel
keyPoints.content: Crude oil is more than just a fuel. It is also an essential feedstock for the chemical industry — the raw material from which plastics, medicines, dyes, detergents, and thousands of other products are made. This is why many chemists argue that burning crude oil as a fuel is wasteful — it destroys a resource that could be used to make high-value products. Alternatives to fossil fuels for energy include: renewable electricity (solar, wind, tidal), hydrogen (produced by electrolysis — especially if using renewable electricity), and biofuels (made from plant material, which absorbs CO₂ as it grows — potentially carbon neutral).
writeBullets: Crude oil is a feedstock: raw material for plastics, medicines, dyes, detergents
Burning oil as fuel destroys this feedstock value
Alternatives for energy: solar, wind, hydrogen, biofuels
Biofuels: from plant material — absorbs CO₂ as it grows → potentially carbon neutral
Hydrogen fuel: produced by electrolysis of water — zero carbon if powered by renewables

keyTerms.def: A raw material used to make other chemical products — crude oil is both a fuel and a feedstock for the chemical industry
A fuel made from plant material (biomass); potentially carbon-neutral as the plants absorb CO₂ as they grow
Producing no net addition of CO₂ to the atmosphere — CO₂ released equals CO₂ absorbed

keyPoints.memTip: Crude oil = fuel + feedstock. If you burn it, you lose the feedstock value. Biofuels are carbon neutral in theory — plant grows (absorbs CO₂) → biofuel burns (releases CO₂) → net = zero. Hydrogen fuel produces only water when used — but making it takes energy.

0.question: Which of the following is a finite resource?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — copper ore is a finite resource. It took millions of years to form in the Earth's crust and exists in limited deposits. Recycling copper reduces the need to mine more.
0.wrongFeedback: Copper ore is a finite resource — it formed in the Earth's crust over millions of years and cannot be replenished. Timber, solar, and wind are renewable (replenished naturally or essentially unlimited). Metal ores, fossil fuels, and non-metallic minerals are the key finite resources in GCSE chemistry.

1.question: What does 'sustainable development' mean in the context of resource use?
options: Using only renewable resources and banning all use of finite resources
Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs
Developing new technologies to extract finite resources more efficiently
Only using resources that are found within a country's own borders
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly — that's the standard definition. Sustainable development doesn't ban finite resource use; it requires managing resources so future generations are not left without.
1.wrongFeedback: Sustainable development means meeting current needs without preventing future generations from meeting theirs. It doesn't require banning all finite resource use — but it does require efficient use, recycling, and developing renewable alternatives so that we don't exhaust resources that future people will need.

commonMistakes: Saying 'water is a finite resource' — water is renewable because it is recycled through the water cycle. However, clean, accessible fresh water can be a scarce resource in some regions.
Saying crude oil is only a fuel — it is also a feedstock for plastics, medicines, dyes, and thousands of other chemicals. AQA expects you to know this distinction.

Confusing carbon neutral with zero carbon — carbon neutral means CO₂ released = CO₂ absorbed (net zero). Zero carbon means no CO₂ released at all. Biofuels are potentially carbon neutral, not zero carbon.
Not giving the full definition of sustainable development — saying 'not wasting resources' is incomplete. The AQA definition includes 'without compromising future generations'.
examTips: For 'evaluate the use of biofuels' questions: benefit = renewable, potentially carbon neutral. Limitation = land use (crops grown for fuel compete with food crops), still releases CO₂ when burned.

For 'why is recycling metals important?' questions: link to finite resource argument (ores will run out), energy saving (recycling uses less energy than extraction from ore), and reduced habitat destruction from mining.
Sustainable development definition: 'meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs'. Quote this exactly in an exam.
When asked why burning crude oil is wasteful: state that crude oil is also a feedstock for making plastics and chemicals — burning it destroys this valuable resource.
revisionCardBullets: Finite resources: formed over millions of years, limited quantity — fossil fuels, metal ores
Renewable resources: replenished naturally in a human lifetime — timber, solar, wind, crops
Sustainable development: meet present needs without compromising future generations
Crude oil: both a fuel AND a feedstock for plastics, medicines, dyes
Burning crude oil wastes its value as a chemical feedstock
Biofuels: from plant material — potentially carbon neutral (CO₂ absorbed as plants grow)
Hydrogen fuel: from electrolysis of water — zero carbon if powered by renewables
Recycling: reduces need to extract finite resources, saves energy, reduces habitat destruction
Bioplastics: made from renewable plant material instead of crude oil
